Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I'd try to dedicate around 18 hours a week, but it never felt like enough or like I was doing it right. I'm the kind of person who needs to physically do things to learn, so I'd often end up rewriting notes by hand or pacing around my room while reviewing concepts. The routine existed on paper, but my motivation to stick to it was pretty low most days.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I'd tell myself I'd study at the same times each week, but then something would come up with my extracurriculars or I'd just feel too drained to care. Deadlines were really the main thing that got me moving. When there wasn't something urgent, I'd often just scroll through the LMS without really accomplishing much.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or just habit at this point. I logged in pretty frequently, like 37 times over the course, but half the time I was just checking if anything new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just because I felt guilty about not studying, even if I didn't actually do anything productive once I was there.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became automatic after a while. It was part of my morning routine to check the LMS with my coffee, even if I was just skimming. The peer support group I was assigned to helped with that because I felt some obligation to check the discussion boards. It became habit, but not necessarily a productive one—more like a reflex.
Q5: The videos were actually helpful because I could watch them while doing other things or rewatch parts I didn't get. I watched almost 288 minutes of video content, which is a lot for me. The discussion posts were useful too because hearing how other students explained things in their own words sometimes clicked better than the lectures. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything I could interact with felt more valuable.
Q6: The reading materials I mostly ignored or skimmed really quickly. Sitting and reading dense text just doesn't work for my brain—I zone out after a few paragraphs. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped because they felt like busywork when I was already stressed about the required assignments. If something didn't feel immediately necessary, it got pushed to the bottom of my list and usually stayed there.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam. Anything that felt abstract or theoretical without clear application made me bored almost instantly. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers or actually do something hands-on rather than just passively absorb information. The moment something felt like it was just checking a box, I checked out mentally.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Like, I knew they might help, but with my anxiety about the required stuff, I couldn't justify spending time on extras. Required activities had this weight to them that forced me to engage even when I didn't want to. Optional ones just became another source of guilt when I didn't do them, which didn't help my stress levels at all.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and PDFs early on and then studied from those on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I also made physical flashcards and would quiz myself while walking around campus. My notes from class were probably my most-used resource, and none of that showed up in any system. I learn better when I can touch and manipulate things.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd print out practice problems and work through them at my desk, or I'd meet up with classmates from my peer support group to work through concepts together in person. We'd use a whiteboard in the library and spend hours on material, but to the system, it looked like I wasn't doing anything.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that was it—I'd use it repeatedly offline. There was one module I must have reviewed five or six times from my downloads, but the LMS only registered that initial access. It's just more convenient to have things saved locally, especially when the wifi in my dorm is unreliable.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt like I was drowning even though I was technically keeping up with attendance. There were days when just opening the LMS made my chest tight. My extracurriculars were also demanding more time, and I felt pulled in too many directions. The boredom mixed with stress is a weird combination—like I didn't care but also cared too much.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from practice or overwhelmed with other responsibilities, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely sometimes. The fatigue was real—I'd watch a video and realize I absorbed nothing because my brain was fried. My LMS usage looked consistent, but the quality of that engagement was all over the place.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the lecture video playing while doing something else entirely, or I'd click through readings just to mark them complete. There were moments where I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out. It's like my body was going through the motions while my brain was somewhere else dealing with anxiety.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments constantly, even knowing they were worth a lot of points. The anxiety made starting them feel impossible—like this huge mountain I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same dread. Some discussion posts I also delayed because formulating my thoughts felt exhausting when my motivation was so low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done half of what I did. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly kick into gear, cramming everything into the last few days. It's not healthy, and my assignment completion rate shows it—75% isn't great. But that pressure was the only thing cutting through the boredom and low motivation.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When it was encouraging, I'd feel a brief boost, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety spike and I'd avoid that topic for a while. The peer support helped because getting informal feedback from classmates felt less threatening than official grades. But honestly, feedback rarely changed my actual behavior in the long run.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which meant I knew what to expect but also found it kind of monotonous. Having regular weekly modules gave me a framework, but it also made everything blend together into this gray routine. The structure helped me not fall completely behind, but it didn't do anything to spark my interest or make me want to engage more deeply.
Q19: The video content being broken into shorter segments helped because I could handle 15 minutes at a time. But some of the assignment instructions were confusing, which made my anxiety worse and caused me to procrastinate. The discussion requirements were actually good for me because the peer support aspect forced some social accountability. The lack of hands-on activities made it harder for someone like me who learns by doing.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is anxious or lost. You keep logging in, watching videos, reading forums, trying to understand—but when it comes to actually producing work, there's this block. Maybe they're perfectionists who can't submit something imperfect, or maybe the assignments feel disconnected from what they've been studying. High activity can mask a lot of internal struggle.
Q21: That makes sense to me because I know people who barely touch the LMS but study from textbooks or form study groups outside the system. They might be efficient learners who don't need to rewatch videos multiple times. Or they could be working from downloaded materials like I do. The LMS doesn't capture everything, so low activity doesn't necessarily mean low effort or understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They miss offline study, emotional state, and whether the time spent was meaningful. A log might show I watched a video, but it can't show that I was too anxious to absorb any of it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just completing tasks—it's wanting to understand, being curious, feeling like the effort matters. For me, real engagement happens when I can physically interact with material or discuss it with peers. Just clicking through modules isn't engagement; it's survival mode.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything I did, or how often I studied with my peer support group in person. It wouldn't capture the flashcards I made, the notes I rewrote, or the conversations that actually helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show that despite being bored and stressed, I kept showing up—even when every login felt like a small battle against my own brain.
